title: downsampleForContainer: bitwise `ratio | 0` truncation can under-select downsample factor when floating-point division yields a value just below a power-of-two
description: The expression `ratio | 0` converts the float ratio to an int32 by truncation (floor toward zero). For integer image and container dimensions that are exact multiples, JS division is exact (e.g., 4000/1000 = 4.0). However for non-multiple integer pairs the result is an IEEE 754 float that could be just below a power-of-two (e.g., a ratio mathematically equal to 4.0 but computed as 3.99999... in extended precision before rounding). In that case `(3.999|0) = 3`, `floor(log2(3)) = 1`, `1<<1 = 2` — returning downsample 2 instead of 4. The correct guard is `Math.floor(ratio)` which gives the same result for exact values and avoids surprise for near-integer ratios. In practice integer pixel dimensions make this unlikely but not impossible for dimensions like 4001px image in 1000px container where ratio=4.001 should give 4.
evidence: The finder's example of 4001/1000=4.001 is self-refuting: `4.001|0=4`, `Math.clz32(4)=29`, `log2=2`, returns 4 — correct. IEEE 754 double-precision division of two integers never produces a value like 3.9999... that rounds down to 3 for reasonable photographic pixel dimensions; the claim requires a float that is simultaneously the mathematical floor minus an epsilon, which does not arise in JS integer division of small 32-bit values.

title: applyDecodePolicy and applyEncodePolicy: `if (!p)` guard is unreachable dead code for typed callers and misleading for untyped callers
description: The functions take a `DecodePolicyName`/`EncodePolicyName` parameter, so TypeScript prevents invalid names at compile time. The `decodePolicies` and `encodePolicies` objects are `as const`, so every valid key lookup returns a non-nullish object — `p` can never be falsy at runtime for any name satisfying the type. The guard fires only when called from untyped JS with an invalid string, but in that path the lookup on an `as const` object returns `undefined`, making `if (!p)` technically correct but confusing since it appears the check is necessary. The real issue is that the same guard is absent from `isDecodePolicyName`/`isEncodePolicyName` call sites, so callers who use the guard functions would never reach `applyDecodePolicy` with a bad name anyway. This is cosmetically dead code that could mislead reviewers into thinking the `as const` lookup can fail.
evidence: The guard is NOT dead for untyped JavaScript callers — test case 6 (line 65) explicitly tests `applyDecodePolicy('bogus' as any, ...)` and asserts the RangeError fires, meaning the guard is the live safety net for dynamic callers. The finding's own description says 'fires only when called from untyped JS with an invalid string' which confirms it IS reachable; calling it 'dead code' or 'misleading' is incorrect — it is the intended runtime boundary for non-TypeScript consumers.

title: downsampleForContainer test suite missing: mismatched-aspect-ratio case, ratio-exactly-3 boundary, and non-square containers
description: The test suite covers square-ratio cases (4:3 image in 4:3 container so both axes tie) and zero/negative guards. It does not test the case where the two axes produce different ratios and the min picks the limiting dimension (e.g., 4000x3000 image in 500x1500 container — width ratio 8 but height ratio 2 forces return 2). It also does not test ratio=3 (not a power-of-two): imageW=3000, containerW=1000 -> ratio=3 -> log2=1 -> return 2, which may surprise callers expecting 4. Adding these cases would pin the floor-log2 behavior and catch any future regression from a change to the clamping logic.
evidence: The test file at lines 133-154 confirms: all 6 helper-math test cases use proportional dimensions where both axes give the same ratio; no test exercises an asymmetric container (e.g. wide image, narrow container) where `Math.min` would pick a different axis, nor ratio=3 (non-power-of-two). These cases are genuinely missing and would detect regressions in the min-axis selection logic.

title: downsampleForContainer return type is structurally unchecked: no assertion that result is 1|2|4|8
description: The function returns `(1 << Math.min(log2, 3)) as Downsample`. The `as Downsample` is a type assertion, not a runtime check. If `log2` were ever negative (which cannot happen given the `ratio >= 2` guard above, but could happen if the guard logic changed), `1 << negative` produces 0 in JS due to unsigned-right-shift semantics mapping negative shifts to large positives modulo 32 — actually `1 << -1 = -2147483648` in JS. A small runtime assertion or a unit-test checking the type invariant would catch this class of regression.
code_excerpt:
const log2 = 31 - Math.clz32(ratio | 0);
  return (1 << Math.min(log2, 3)) as Downsample;
evidence: The NaN/Infinity overflow path (confirmed in errors-2 and errors-3) already produces a return value of -2147483648 which violates the `1|2|4|8` Downsample contract; the `as Downsample` cast silently lies. A runtime invariant check at the return site would catch both the NaN path and any future arithmetic regression, and is directly motivated by real bugs in the current implementation.

title: applyDecodePolicy null-guard on `p` is unreachable due to TypeScript constraint but masks future runtime risk
description: The `if (!p)` guard at line 57 can never be true: `name` is typed as `DecodePolicyName` and `decodePolicies` is a `const satisfies Record<DecodePolicyName, DecodePolicy>`, so every valid name is always present. When callers bypass TypeScript (e.g. via `as any` or a dynamic string cast through `isDecodePolicyName`), the guard would only fire at runtime after a type-unsound call.
evidence: The guard is reachable for untyped/dynamic callers and is explicitly exercised by test case 6 (`applyDecodePolicy('bogus' as any, ...)`) — it correctly throws RangeError. Calling it a 'dead guard' is inaccurate; it is the intended runtime safety net. The description's own logic ('the guard would only fire at runtime after a type-unsound call') confirms the guard is intentional and useful, not dead.

title: downsampleForContainer returns wrong result for non-integer or very large ratio due to truncating bitwise cast
description: `ratio | 0` truncates a float to a 32-bit signed integer. For extremely large images (e.g. imageW=4_294_967_296, containerW=1), `ratio` overflows a 32-bit int and `ratio | 0` becomes 0, causing `Math.clz32(0)` to return 32, so `log2 = 31 - 32 = -1`, and `1 << -1` evaluates to `1 << 31 = -2147483648` as a signed int. The cast `as Downsample` then returns a large negative number rather than clamping to 8.
evidence: Verified against source: `ratio | 0` is a 32-bit signed truncation — for ratio > 2^31 it wraps to 0 (or negative), `Math.clz32(0)=32`, `log2=-1`, `1 << Math.min(-1,3)` = `1 << -1` = `-2147483648` in JS 32-bit arithmetic. The function has no upper-bound clamp on ratio before the bitwise cast; the guard only checks `imageW <= 0` not `imageW > Number.MAX_SAFE_INTEGER`. The fix is `Math.min(31, Math.floor(Math.log2(ratio)))` or clamping ratio to 2^31-1 before the bitwise op.

title: downsampleForContainer does not guard against NaN or Infinity inputs
description: `imageW <= 0` is false for `NaN` (NaN comparisons always return false), so passing `NaN` for any dimension bypasses the guard and proceeds into the ratio computation. `Math.min(NaN, ...)` returns NaN, `NaN < 2` is false, `NaN | 0` is 0, leading to `Math.clz32(0) = 32`, `log2 = -1`, and ultimately `1 << -1 = -2147483648` returned as `Downsample`. Similarly `Infinity / Infinity` produces NaN. The guard should be `!Number.isFinite(x) || x <= 0` for all four parameters.
evidence: Verified: `NaN <= 0` is `false` in JS, so NaN bypasses the guard at line 126. The chain `Math.min(NaN/x, NaN/y)=NaN`, `NaN < 2=false`, `NaN|0=0`, `Math.clz32(0)=32`, `31-32=-1`, `1<<-1=-2147483648` is mechanically correct. The guard at line 126 uses `<= 0` which does not catch NaN or Infinity inputs; the fix `!Number.isFinite(x) || x <= 0` would address both.

title: applyDecodePolicy and applyEncodePolicy accept null base without throwing, silently spreading null
description: Both `applyDecodePolicy` and `applyEncodePolicy` spread `base` using `{ ...base, ... }`. If a caller passes `null` or `undefined` as `base` (possible via `as any` or a dynamic dispatch path), spreading `null` in JavaScript silently produces an empty object (`{ ...null }` is `{}`), so the function does not throw but returns a policy-only object without the caller's intended fields.
evidence: The behavior described is technically correct (`{ ...null }` = `{}` in JS), but the null path also triggers `base.progressionTarget` property access on null at line 62, which DOES throw a TypeError — so it does not silently succeed. The specific failure scenario (silent data loss) doesn't hold because `base.progressionTarget` access on null throws before the spread completes. Whether this matters depends on JS caller patterns not visible in this package.

title: Policy errors use bare RangeError with no error code, making programmatic handling harder for callers
description: Both `applyDecodePolicy` and `applyEncodePolicy` throw `new RangeError(message)` with a human-readable string. Callers that need to distinguish a policy-name error from other RangeErrors must parse the message string, which is fragile.
evidence: Confirmed in source: both policy functions throw bare `new RangeError(message)` with no `.code` property or custom subclass. The rest of the codebase (jxl-core types) also uses plain Error subclasses without codes. Adding a `code` property would be a small, non-breaking improvement enabling reliable programmatic error handling.

title: downsampleForContainer tests do not cover NaN or very-large-ratio inputs that cause integer overflow
description: The helper math tests (cases 1-6) verify zero/negative guard and normal ratios, but there is no test for NaN inputs or for ratios large enough to overflow `ratio | 0` (> 2^31). These are the exact inputs that expose the integer-overflow and NaN-propagation bugs identified in errors-2 and errors-3.
evidence: Test file lines 133-154 confirm no NaN, Infinity, or ratio>2^31 test cases exist. The real bugs in errors-2 (overflow) and errors-3 (NaN bypass) are confirmed independently, so these missing tests are directly motivated by real defects that would be caught if the tests existed.

title: downsampleForContainer returns an invalid Downsample cast when ratio|0 overflows to 0 (NaN or Infinity input)
description: If containerW or containerH is passed as Infinity or NaN (both pass the `<= 0` guard), `Math.min(imageW/containerW, imageH/containerH)` yields NaN. `NaN | 0` = 0, and `Math.clz32(0)` = 32, so `31 - 32 = -1`. Then `1 << Math.min(-1, 3)` = `1 << -1` = `-2147483648` in JS (bitwise ops use 32-bit signed). The `as Downsample` cast silently lies and a value of -2147483648 is returned to callers who expect `1|2|4|8`.
evidence: Same root cause as errors-2 and errors-3, confirmed against source. The guard `<= 0` does not catch NaN (NaN <= 0 is false), Infinity/Infinity=NaN bypasses the check, and `NaN|0=0` leads to `Math.clz32(0)=32`, `31-32=-1`, `1<<-1=-2147483648`. This is a concrete contract violation where the return type claims `1|2|4|8` but the function can return -2147483648.

title: applyDecodePolicy silently propagates an invalid caller-supplied downsample value (e.g. 3, 5, 7) into DecodeOptions
description: DecodeOptions.downsample is typed `1 | 2 | 4 | 8` but applyDecodePolicy does not validate `base.downsample` before accepting it. A caller passing `downsample: 3` (not a valid power of two) will have that value win via `base.downsample ?? p.downsample` and land in the output unchecked. Since this is a public entry point that composes caller options with policy defaults, the function is the natural validation boundary; without it the error manifests only at the WASM bridge.
evidence: The code at line 66-68 confirms no validation of the downsample value. However, DecodeOptions.downsample is typed as `1|2|4|8` in TypeScript, so passing 3 requires deliberate type-unsound code. Whether this matters depends on whether JS/dynamic callers are an intended audience. The finding is real for dynamic callers but the severity is overstated for a typed TS package — classifying as uncertain since the package's intended caller profile is unclear.

title: Dead `if (!p)` guards in applyDecodePolicy and applyEncodePolicy are misleading and not tested
description: Both applyDecodePolicy (line 57) and applyEncodePolicy (line 93) contain `if (!p) throw new RangeError(...)` after indexing a frozen const-satisfies object with a `DecodePolicyName`/`EncodePolicyName`-typed parameter. TypeScript guarantees `p` is always defined at these call sites; the guard is unreachable under correct typing.
evidence: The finding claims the guard is 'not tested' but test case 6 (policy.test.ts line 65) explicitly calls `applyDecodePolicy('bogus' as any, ...)` and asserts the RangeError fires correctly — the guard is tested. The finder is also wrong that the guard is 'dead': it is the runtime safety net for plain-JS callers or cast-bypassed callers, not dead code.

title: DecodePolicyName enum and decodePolicies table do not include a 'full' or 'hq' policy, leaving callers with no policy for non-progressive full-quality viewer
description: QUESTION: The decode policy table has 'viewer' (emitEveryPass:true, target:final) and 'export' (emitEveryPass:false, target:final) but no named policy for a high-quality viewer that wants final quality without intermediate passes yet is still interactive-priority.
evidence: This is a design question flagged by the finder itself as 'low' confidence. The existing 'export' policy already covers final-quality, no-intermediates, visible-priority (emitEveryPass:false, progressionTarget:final, priority:visible) — which is exactly the described 'non-progressive full-quality viewer' use case. The finding misreads 'export' as a batch/offline-only policy, but the priority field 'visible' contradicts that. No missing policy.

category: no contract testing / schema validation at boundary
title: No test covers round-trip invariant: applyDecodePolicy output is a valid DecodeOptions accepted by the scheduler/session without further transformation
description: The test suite validates the policy logic in isolation (caller-wins, gap-fill, idempotence) but no test exercises the contract that the output of applyDecodePolicy is structurally valid as a DecodeOptions accepted by jxl-session or jxl-scheduler without further coercion.
evidence: The test file confirms all tests are self-contained within jxl-policy with no integration against jxl-session or jxl-scheduler types. The policy output shape drifting from DecodeOptions (e.g., if DecodeOptions gains a required field) would not be caught by these tests until runtime. This is a genuine gap in contract coverage for a package whose entire purpose is to produce valid DecodeOptions/EncodeOptions.

title: Policy tables have no version or changelog mechanism; consumers cannot detect when a policy's defaults change across package versions
description: DecodePolicies and EncodePolicies are frozen but have no semantic version separate from the package version. A consumer pinning to a policy name across package updates (e.g. 'viewer') may silently receive different defaults.
evidence: Standard npm semver already provides this guarantee: breaking policy default changes would warrant a major or minor bump, and consumers pin package versions via package.json. Exporting a separate POLICY_SCHEMA_VERSION constant would add no information that package version doesn't already convey. This is an over-engineering suggestion for a monorepo package.
